textDisplay: Been following the Year 2 videos closely and the bleaching data here is sobering. The north patch comparison maps are really well done. Thank you for making this publicly accessible.
Question - at 14:15 you show the temperature anomaly correlation. Are those degree heating weeks from Coral Reef Watch or a local sensor network?

Both actually. We overlay Coral Reef Watch 5km satellite DHW with our in-situ logger array at four sites. The CRW product lags our loggers by 1-2 days and runs slightly cooler, which we note in the methodology section of the report linked in the description.
Community monitoring team was proud to contribute to this. Our volunteers logged over 600 data points this season. This is what co-management looks like.
Saving this for my marine science unit. The bleaching prevalence map at 10:00 is exactly what I needed to show students that monitoring produces real usable data.
I was one of the 23 volunteers that day. Incredible experience. The pre-dive briefing and the way the team explained the scientific purpose made it feel meaningful, not just busywork.
What coral genera are you outplanting primarily? Montipora capitata or Pocillopora? Curious about survivorship rates so far.

Mostly Montipora capitata from our Kāneʻohe nursery line with some Porites compressa. Survivorship at 12 months is tracking around 68% for M. capitata at the patch sites. Full data in our Q1 2025 progress report at coralfuturesinstitute.org.
Love seeing the community involvement piece. The land and sea are connected - the same stewardship ethic applies. Mahalo for the work.
This is the QGIS tutorial I have been looking for. Every intro GIS video uses urban land use data. Using actual reef transect GPS tracks is so much more relevant for marine students.
The CRS and projection section at 8:00 is where most beginners go wrong. Glad you gave it proper treatment instead of glossing over it. I have spent hours fixing mismatched projections before.

Exactly - projection errors are silent until they are catastrophic. We had a graduate intern once overlay two layers 300 meters apart because of a WGS84 vs NAD83 mismatch. Spent an afternoon on that. The rule is check your CRS before every join.
I work in seagrass monitoring and downloaded QGIS 3.34 specifically for this tutorial series. Already mapped three of our transect sites. Night and day vs the old spreadsheet workflow.
The side by side at 2:00 finally cleared up something I have been unsure about for two seasons. The pale tissue margin vs the totally white skeleton tells the whole story. Thank you.

Good resource. One thing worth adding - growth anomalies and bleaching can co-occur during prolonged thermal stress which makes field ID trickier. Worth noting that photos for CoralNet should capture both conditions separately.
Great point Dr. Park. We flag co-occurring conditions in our photo log with dual tags and train volunteers to photograph each affected colony from at least two angles for exactly that reason.
Watched this before my first community monitoring session and felt genuinely prepared. The surface signals section especially - I practiced with my buddy in shallow water the day before.
The shallow water blackout section is accurate and important. Most people think SWB only happens to experienced freedivers going deep. It absolutely happens at 3 meters if you hyperventilate first.
I had no idea what the stacked bar charts in our session reports meant until this video. Now I actually look forward to getting the post-session summary.

That means a lot to hear. Volunteer-generated data is only valuable if the people collecting it can see the results and understand what they mean. We are working on an annotated report template for community partners this summer.
The tidyr reshape section is exactly where I always get stuck. Watching this cleared up why my benthic data pivots were producing NA rows. The column naming convention was the issue.
Could you do a follow-up on mixed effects models for repeat-measure reef data? Handling the site-level random effect is where I keep getting stuck in lme4.
This is a genuinely balanced treatment of MPA effectiveness research. Most public-facing content cherry-picks the positive studies. The community co-management section reflects the current consensus.

As someone who fishes these waters I appreciate that you talked about community co-management honestly. When local fishers are part of the design MPAs work. When they are not, people work around them.
The land-sea connection data you present here tracks directly with what we see in stream sediment loads during storm events. Really useful to see the downstream reef impact quantified.
We use this video in our stormwater education outreach. The visual at 6:00 showing turbidity plume extent on Google Earth is the most convincing thing we have found for community meetings.
The coin for scale tip is simple and obvious in retrospect but I never thought to do it. My last 50 coral photos are basically useless for size comparison. Starting fresh with this approach.
Shared the planning sheet with our volunteer email list and it was the most downloaded thing we have sent all year. Simple and genuinely useful.
We adapted this PIT method for seagrass beds and it works beautifully. The tape deployment technique you demonstrate here is cleaner than what we were taught in our lab training.

The lunar timing detail here is something most people overlook. I have been night snorkeling in August hoping to catch spawning for years with no luck because the species window is narrower than I realized.
We reference this video in our regional manager onboarding. Understanding DHW and bleaching alert thresholds is foundational and the CRW portal walkthrough here is the clearest one I have found.
The February 2025 event example at the end really grounds this. We activated the protocol that morning and the turbidity at site 3 was the worst we have logged in three years.
The salinity monitoring piece is underrated. Post-storm freshwater influx drops salinity faster than turbidity in some parts of the bay and corals respond to both stressors.

Absolutely right. We have a YSI sonde at the north mooring specifically for that. Salinity can drop 4-6 PSU in the inner bay within hours of a major rain event. The coral response there is different from the outer reef sites and tracking both matters.

As a paddler of 15 years this resonated deeply. The ocean teaches you things no textbook does. The current shift observation around October you describe matches exactly what our crew notices every year.
Used CoralNet for two years and this is the best onboarding tutorial I have seen. The label set configuration section especially - most people configure it wrong on the first try.

I have watched a lot of reef and climate content and this is one of the most honest treatments of the science I have found. You do not downplay the projections but you do not catastrophize either. That balance is rare.

Thank you - that balance is something we think hard about. Eco-grief is real and we see it in our volunteers too. The goal is to be accurate about risk while holding space for what is worth protecting and what resilience still looks like.

Finally a video that actually engages with the critique of the Downs oxybenzone study instead of just citing it as settled. The lab concentration vs field exposure gap is the real issue and you address it directly.

Joined the Kappaphycus removal program at Kāneʻohe last summer. Had no idea what I was looking at when I signed up. Wish I had found this video first. The ID photos here are much clearer than the printed sheet we were given.
The white balance comparison in ambient light vs custom kelp-green white balance at 4:30 sold me. Ordered the PT-059 housing after watching this. My coral photos were always looking too blue.

The herbivore guild findings are encouraging. Seeing parrotfish biomass stable or slightly up across the inner bay is a signal worth noting. They are doing real work keeping macroalgae from overtaking bleached substrate.
Dealt with a COTS aggregation at Molokini last season. The handling protocol you demonstrate here is very close to what DLNR recommended - vinegar injection vs bile salt injection debate aside.
The Montipora capitata vs flabellata comparison is something I have messed up many times. The encrusting vs columnar growth form distinction finally makes sense to me with the side-by-side footage.

The data ownership section should be required reading before anyone joins any citizen science program. I have seen programs where volunteers collected thousands of observations and never received any acknowledgment in publications.
About to sign up for Coral Futures community monitoring. Watched this first. Really glad the program is transparent about how data is used. That section about red flags was eye-opening.

The before-and-after coral cover data at Site 7 is remarkable. Going from urchin barren to measurable live coral cover in 18 months with targeted intervention - that is the kind of result that justifies active management.

Site 7 has been one of our most instructive sites. The urchin and algae removal had to happen in the right sequence - algae removal first, then manage urchin density after natural coral recruits were visible. Sequence mattered more than we expected.
I share this video with every snorkeler who asks me about the white coral they saw. The bleaching explanation at 2:00 is accessible without being dumbed down. Really effective science communication.
Reef Guardians Alliance cross-posted this to our members. The community monitoring contribution section is exactly the kind of recognition that keeps volunteers coming back season after season.
